subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Character Components
- **17** character class tuck boxes, each containing a unique miniature, ability card set, character sheet pad, and personal quest cards
- **6** starting character classes available from the first scenario (Brute, Tinkerer, Spellweaver, Scoundrel, Cragheart, Mindthief)
- **11** locked character classes unlocked by retiring characters and completing personal quests throughout the campaign
- **Character ability cards** — Each class has a unique set of level 1 through level 9 ability cards with top and bottom actions
- **Attack modifier decks** — 1 base modifier deck per player (20 cards each) plus 1 monster modifier deck, with perk stickers to customize

#### Map & Overlay Tiles
- **Map board** — Large world map showing the city of Gloomhaven and surrounding regions, with sticker locations for unlocked scenarios
- **150+ map overlay tiles** — Corridor tiles, room tiles, and irregular dungeon shapes of various sizes used to build each scenario
- **Overlay tokens** — Door overlays (opening/closing), trap overlays (damage/condition), obstacle overlays (blocking terrain), difficult terrain overlays, hazardous terrain overlays, and treasure overlays

#### Monster Components
- **Monster standees** — Over 45 monster types with multiple standees per type, in plastic bases (white for normal, gold/yellow for elite)
- **Monster stat sheets** — Double-sided stat cards for each monster type showing HP, movement, attack, range, and special abilities per level
- **Monster ability cards** — Unique ability deck for each monster type drawn each round to determine monster actions
- **Boss stat cards** — Special oversized stat cards for boss enemies with unique abilities and scaling HP
- **Monster stat sleeves** — Plastic sleeves to track current HP for each active monster using rotating dials

#### Cards & Decks
- **City Event deck** — 80+ city event cards with A/B choices resolved between scenarios while in Gloomhaven
- **Road Event deck** — 60+ road event cards with A/B choices resolved when traveling to a scenario
- **Personal Quest cards** — 24 personal quest cards that define a character's retirement goal
- **Item cards** — Over 150 item cards including head, body, legs, one-hand, two-hand, and small item slots
- **Random Dungeon deck** — Cards for generating random side scenarios outside the campaign

#### Tokens & Tracking
- **Damage tokens** — Numbered tokens (1 and 5 values) for tracking monster and summon HP
- **Condition tokens** — Tokens for Poison, Wound, Immobilize, Disarm, Stun, Muddle, Strengthen, Invisible
- **Money tokens** — Coin tokens (1-gold values) dropped by defeated monsters and looted by players
- **XP tokens** — Experience point tokens earned during scenarios for performing certain actions
- **Element tokens** — 6 element discs (Fire, Ice, Air, Earth, Light, Dark) with an element board tracking Strong, Waning, and Inert states
- **HP/XP dials** — Rotating dial trackers for each player to track current HP and XP during a scenario

#### Books & Campaign Materials
- **Scenario book** — Contains ~95 scenarios with map layouts, monster placements, special rules, and narrative text
- **Rulebook** — Comprehensive rules reference covering all core mechanics, monster AI, and campaign progression
- **Town Records book** — Sealed sections of story content unlocked by achievements and campaign events
- **Sticker sheets** — Map stickers for unlocked scenarios, enhancement stickers for ability cards, and achievement stickers
- **Achievement sheet** — Tracks global and party achievements that affect which scenarios are available and event outcomes

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Scenario Selection
- **Choose a scenario** — Select an available scenario from the scenario book (Scenario 1: Black Barrow is the starting scenario for new campaigns)
- **Read introduction** — Read the scenario's flavor text and any special rules before setting up the map
- **Check requirements** — Verify any required achievements or prerequisites listed for the scenario are met

#### Map Tile Placement
- **Place starting room** — Lay out only the first room's map tiles as shown in the scenario diagram; do not reveal other rooms yet
- **Door overlays** — Place closed door overlay tiles between rooms as indicated in the scenario layout
- **Trap overlays** — Place trap tokens in hexes marked with trap icons; note damage value and condition from scenario rules
- **Obstacle overlays** — Place obstacle tokens (rocks, bookcases, tables, etc.) in marked hexes; these block movement and line of sight
- **Hazardous terrain** — Place hazardous terrain overlays where indicated; characters entering these hexes suffer damage
- **Difficult terrain** — Place difficult terrain overlays where indicated; entering these hexes costs 2 movement points
- **Treasure overlays** — Place numbered treasure tiles in marked hexes; contents are listed in the scenario book treasure index

#### Monster Placement
- **Starting room monsters** — Place monster standees in hexes marked in the first room only (white base = normal, gold base = elite)
- **Monster stat cards** — Set the monster stat sheets for all monster types present in the scenario to the correct scenario level
- **Monster ability decks** — Shuffle the ability card deck for each monster type appearing in the scenario
- **Monster HP tracking** — Insert stat cards into sleeves and set HP dials for each placed monster standee

#### Element Board & Supply
- **Element board** — Place the element board nearby with all 6 element tokens set to Inert (bottom position)
- **Damage tokens** — Place damage token supply within reach of all players
- **Condition tokens** — Sort condition tokens by type and place them in the shared supply
- **Money tokens** — Place money token supply nearby for monster drops and loot

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Choose Your Class
- **Starting classes** — 6 classes are available at the start of a new campaign: Brute (tank/melee), Tinkerer (support/ranged), Spellweaver (elemental mage), Scoundrel (melee DPS/rogue), Cragheart (ranged/obstacle manipulation), Mindthief (melee/crowd control)
- **Class tuck box** — Take your chosen class's tuck box containing the miniature, ability cards, character sheet pad, and modifiers
- **Character sheet** — Fill out a new character sheet: write your character name, mark level 1, set starting gold, and note your max HP
- **Personal quest** — Draw 2 personal quest cards and keep 1 (return the other to the box); this defines your character's retirement goal

#### Ability Card Selection
- **Level 1 cards** — Take all level 1 and level X ability cards for your class
- **Hand size** — Each class has a fixed hand size (e.g., Brute = 10, Spellweaver = 8, Tinkerer = 12, Scoundrel = 9, Cragheart = 11, Mindthief = 10)
- **Select hand** — Choose exactly your hand size number of cards from your available ability cards to bring into the scenario
- **Card review** — Read each card's top action and bottom action carefully; plan synergies between cards before finalizing your hand
- **Unused cards** — Set aside any ability cards not selected for this scenario; they remain available for future hand selections

#### Attack Modifier Deck
- **Base deck** — Take the standard 20-card attack modifier deck: six +0 cards, five +1 cards, five -1 cards, one +2 card, one -2 card, one ×0 (Miss) card, one ×2 (Critical) card
- **Perks** — If your character has earned perks from leveling up or completing battle goals, modify your deck accordingly (remove negative cards, add rolling modifiers, add element infusions, etc.)
- **Shuffle deck** — Shuffle your attack modifier deck and place it face down near your play area

#### Items & Equipment
- **Starting gold** — New level 1 characters begin with 30 gold to spend on items from the available supply
- **Item slots** — Each character can equip: 1 head item, 1 body item, 1 legs item, up to 2 one-hand items (or 1 two-hand item), and a number of small items equal to half your level rounded up
- **Item purchase** — Buy items from the Gloomhaven shop; available items expand as the campaign progresses and prosperity increases
- **Item cards** — Place purchased item cards face up near your character sheet; these are available for use during the scenario

#### HP & Battle Goal
- **HP dial** — Set your HP dial to your class's maximum hit points for your current level (listed on the character sheet)
- **Battle goal** — Draw 2 battle goal cards and secretly keep 1; completing this optional objective during the scenario earns a perk checkmark

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### Road Event (If Traveling)
- **Draw road event** — If the scenario is not in Gloomhaven or linked directly from the previous scenario, draw the top card of the Road Event deck
- **Read event** — Read the narrative text on the front of the card aloud to the party
- **Choose A or B** — The party collectively decides option A or option B, then flip the card to resolve the chosen outcome
- **Apply consequences** — Effects may include gaining/losing gold, gaining/losing HP before the scenario, gaining conditions, gaining/losing reputation, or adding/removing event cards
- **Return or remove** — Some events are returned to the bottom of the deck; others are removed from the game permanently

#### City Event (Between Scenarios)
- **Draw city event** — When in Gloomhaven between scenarios, draw the top card of the City Event deck before your next scenario
- **Resolve identically** — Read front, choose A or B, flip and resolve; city events may unlock items, change prosperity, or trigger story consequences
- **Prosperity effects** — Some events increase or decrease Gloomhaven's prosperity level, which determines shop item availability and starting character level

#### Character Placement
- **Starting hexes** — Place character miniatures in the designated starting hexes shown by the scenario diagram (marked with player icons)
- **Choose positions** — If multiple starting hexes are available, players decide collectively who starts where; positioning matters for first-room tactics
- **Verify HP** — Confirm all players have HP dials set to maximum (adjusted for any road event damage)

#### Final Preparations
- **Shuffle monster decks** — Ensure all monster ability decks are shuffled
- **Shuffle modifier decks** — Ensure all player and monster attack modifier decks are shuffled
- **Review scenario rules** — Re-read any special scenario rules, win conditions, and section links
- **Begin round 1** — All players simultaneously select their first 2 ability cards and the first round begins

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Round Overview
- **Simultaneous selection** — At the start of each round, all players secretly and simultaneously select 2 ability cards from their hand
- **Declare initiative** — Each player announces the initiative value printed on one of their two selected cards (this determines turn order)
- **Long rest option** — Instead of selecting 2 cards, a player may declare a long rest (initiative is automatically 99, meaning they go last)
- **Monster initiative** — Flip the top card of each active monster type's ability deck to reveal their initiative value and actions for the round
- **Sort turn order** — Arrange all players and monster groups from lowest initiative to highest; ties between players resolved by the players, ties with monsters broken by the players choosing who goes first

#### Character Turn
- **Play 2 cards** — On your turn, you must play the top half of one selected card and the bottom half of the other card (in any order)
- **Default actions** — Instead of a card's printed action, you may always use a default "Attack 2" for any top half or a default "Move 2" for any bottom half
- **Action order** — You choose which of your two half-actions to perform first; fully resolve one before starting the other
- **Discard after use** — After both half-actions are resolved, place both cards in your discard pile (unless an action has the loss icon ✕, in which case that card goes to your lost pile)
- **End of turn** — After resolving both actions, apply any end-of-turn effects, then play passes to the next entity in initiative order

#### Monster Turn
- **Ability card** — All monsters of a given type act using the same revealed ability card (e.g., all Bandit Guards use the same card)
- **Elite first** — Elite monsters of a type act before normal monsters of that type
- **Individual focus** — Each monster individually determines its focus (which enemy to target) based on proximity and the ability card's attack/move values
- **Resolve sequentially** — Resolve each individual monster's full turn before moving to the next monster of that type

#### End of Round
- **Element waning** — Any element tokens in the Strong column move to Waning; any in Waning move to Inert
- **Round tracker** — Advance any round-tracking effects if applicable to the scenario
- **Shuffle check** — If any attack modifier deck or monster ability deck had its shuffle icon (two arrows) triggered this round, shuffle that deck now
- **Check scenario end** — Verify whether the scenario's victory or defeat conditions have been met

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Move Actions
- **Move X** — Move your character up to X hexes on the map; you do not have to use all movement points
- **Cannot enter enemies** — You may never move into a hex occupied by an enemy figure
- **Move through allies** — You may move through hexes occupied by allied figures but cannot end movement there
- **Obstacles block** — You cannot move into or through hexes containing obstacles unless an ability specifically allows it
- **Difficult terrain** — Entering a difficult terrain hex costs 2 movement points instead of 1
- **Hazardous terrain** — Entering a hazardous terrain hex deals damage as specified by the scenario (usually suffers the listed trap damage)
- **Traps** — Entering a hex with a trap triggers it: suffer the listed damage/condition, then remove the trap token from the map
- **Doors** — Moving onto a closed door hex opens the door immediately, revealing the next room (see Special Mechanics)
- **Jump** — A Jump movement ignores all intervening figures, obstacles, traps, and hazardous/difficult terrain; only the destination hex matters
- **Flying** — Flying figures ignore all movement restrictions from terrain and figures; they may end movement on obstacles or traps without triggering them

#### Attack Actions
- **Attack X** — Perform an attack with base value X against one target within range
- **Melee attacks** — Range is not specified or listed as range 1; target must be in an adjacent hex
- **Ranged attacks** — Range Y means you can target any enemy within Y hexes with line of sight; attacking at range while adjacent to any enemy gives you Disadvantage
- **Attack modifier draw** — Draw the top card of your attack modifier deck and apply it: add/subtract the value, or apply ×0 (Miss = 0 damage) or ×2 (Critical = double base)
- **Rolling modifiers** — Some modifier cards have a rolling icon; draw additional cards until a non-rolling card is drawn, then sum all rolling values
- **Target X** — "Target X" means you attack X separate enemies within range (draw a separate modifier for each target)
- **Advantage** — Draw 2 modifier cards and use the better one (stronger positive or less negative)
- **Disadvantage** — Draw 2 modifier cards and use the worse one (weaker positive or more negative)
- **Pierce X** — Ignore X points of the target's Shield value
- **Damage calculation** — Final damage = (base attack + modifier card value + bonuses) - target's Shield value; minimum 0 damage

#### Conditions
- **Poison** — Target gains Poison token; all sources of damage against this figure deal +1 damage; removed by any Heal effect (heal is consumed removing Poison, no HP restored)
- **Wound** — Target gains Wound token; at the start of each of the target's turns, it suffers 1 damage; removed by any Heal effect (heal is consumed removing Wound, no HP restored)
- **Immobilize** — Target cannot perform any Move abilities on their next turn; removed at end of the affected turn
- **Stun** — Target loses their entire next turn (no actions at all); removed at end of the lost turn; the most powerful condition
- **Muddle** — Target has Disadvantage on all attacks on their next turn; removed at end of the affected turn
- **Strengthen** — Target has Advantage on all attacks on their next turn; removed at end of the affected turn
- **Invisible** — Target cannot be focused or targeted by enemies; removed at end of the affected character's next turn; does not prevent AoE or retaliate
- **Disarm** — Target cannot perform any Attack abilities on their next turn; removed at end of the affected turn
- **Curse** — Add a ×0 (Curse) card to the target's attack modifier deck; removed when the Curse card is drawn
- **Bless** — Add a ×2 (Bless) card to the target's attack modifier deck; removed when the Bless card is drawn

#### Elements
- **Generating elements** — When an action shows an element icon in the ability, that element is created: move that element's token to Strong on the element board at the end of your turn
- **Consuming elements** — Some actions show an element icon with a consume arrow; you may consume that element (move it to Inert) to gain the enhanced effect listed
- **Six elements** — Fire, Ice, Air, Earth, Light, and Dark; each functions identically in terms of the Strong → Waning → Inert cycle
- **Element timing** — You cannot generate and consume the same element on the same turn; generated elements become available for consumption on subsequent turns
- **Waning cycle** — At end of each round, Strong elements move to Waning, and Waning elements move to Inert
- **Shared resource** — Elements are shared among all figures; monsters can also generate and consume elements

#### Other Actions
- **Heal X** — Restore X hit points to target (self or ally within range); cannot exceed maximum HP; first removes Poison or Wound if present (consuming the heal with no HP gain)
- **Shield X** — Reduce each incoming attack's damage by X for the duration specified (usually until end of your next turn); applied after attack modifier
- **Retaliate X** — When attacked by an adjacent enemy (melee retaliate) or within specified range (ranged retaliate), deal X damage back to the attacker; does not trigger attack modifiers
- **Loot X** — Pick up all money tokens and treasure tiles within range X of your hex (loot 1 = your hex and all adjacent hexes); money tokens = gold value, treasure tiles = scenario book reward
- **Push X / Pull X** — Force a target X hexes away from you (Push) or toward you (Pull); target cannot be pushed/pulled through walls or obstacles
- **Summon** — Place a summoned ally figure in an adjacent empty hex; summons have their own stat card, act on your initiative with a basic AI, and are controlled by the summoning player
- **Items** — Use equipped item cards for their listed effects; items may be used once per scenario (consumed), once per long rest (spent then refreshed), or passively (always active)

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Card Loss & Exhaustion
- **Loss actions (✕)** — Ability cards marked with the ✕ (loss) icon go to your lost pile after use; they cannot be recovered for the rest of the scenario
- **Exhaustion trigger** — If at the start of a round you cannot play 2 cards from your hand (because you have 0 or 1 cards), you are exhausted
- **Exhaustion from damage** — When you take damage, you may lose 1 card from your hand (or 2 from your discard) instead of taking the damage; if you cannot, take the full damage
- **Exhausted = eliminated** — An exhausted character is removed from the scenario immediately; they keep XP and gold earned so far but cannot participate further
- **Party exhaustion** — If all characters are exhausted before the scenario objective is complete, the scenario is failed
- **Hand size is your timer** — Your hand size and how you manage loss cards determine how many rounds you can last; larger hand sizes (Tinkerer at 12) allow more endurance than smaller ones (Spellweaver at 8)

#### Short Rest
- **When to rest** — You may take a short rest at the end of any round (not during card selection)
- **Mechanic** — Pick up all cards from your discard pile, then randomly lose one card from among them to the lost pile; the rest return to your hand
- **Reroll option** — If you don't like the randomly lost card, you may suffer 1 damage to randomly lose a different card instead (only once per short rest)
- **No turn skip** — Short resting does not cost you a turn; you still play normally the next round

#### Long Rest
- **When to rest** — Declared during card selection instead of choosing 2 cards; your initiative is automatically 99
- **Mechanic** — On your turn (initiative 99), pick up all cards from your discard pile, choose one to lose to the lost pile, and the rest return to your hand
- **Heal 2** — You also heal 2 HP when you long rest
- **Refresh items** — All spent (flipped) items are refreshed (flipped back to available) during a long rest
- **No actions** — You do not play any ability cards on a long rest turn; your entire turn is the rest

#### Attack Modifier Deck
- **Standard deck composition** — 20 cards: 6× (+0), 5× (+1), 5× (-1), 1× (+2), 1× (-2), 1× (×0 Miss), 1× (×2 Critical)
- **Draw one per attack** — Every single attack you make requires drawing one attack modifier card (or more if rolling modifiers appear)
- **Reshuffle trigger** — When the ×0 (Miss) or ×2 (Critical) card is drawn, shuffle the entire deck at the end of the round
- **Perk upgrades** — Each class has a unique perk list; when you earn perk checkmarks (from battle goals or leveling), you permanently modify your deck by removing bad cards or adding powerful new ones
- **Monster deck** — Monsters share a single attack modifier deck with the same standard composition; it is not customized

#### Monster AI
- **Ability card** — Each round, a monster ability card is drawn for each monster type, dictating their actions and initiative
- **Focus determination** — Each monster focuses on the enemy it can reach and attack with the least movement; this is its primary target
- **Tie-breaking** — If multiple enemies are equidistant, the monster focuses on the one with the lower initiative value
- **Movement** — Monsters move the minimum number of hexes needed to attack their focused target; they will not move if they can already attack
- **No path = no move** — If a monster cannot reach any enemy to attack (even with maximum movement), it does not move
- **Monster conditions** — Monsters are affected by conditions identically to players (Stun, Poison, Wound, etc.)
- **Named monsters** — Some scenarios feature named monsters with unique stat cards and special rules

#### Doors & Room Reveals
- **Opening doors** — Any figure entering a closed door hex opens it immediately, pausing the current turn
- **Reveal new room** — Place all map tiles, overlay tokens, monsters, and treasure for the newly revealed room as shown in the scenario book
- **New monster initiative** — Newly placed monsters act on the initiative of their type's already-drawn ability card; if no card was drawn for that type, draw one now
- **Resume turn** — After the new room is set up, the figure that opened the door continues their remaining actions

#### Leveling Up
- **XP thresholds** — Level 2 at 45 XP, Level 3 at 95 XP, Level 4 at 150 XP, Level 5 at 210 XP, Level 6 at 275 XP, Level 7 at 345 XP, Level 8 at 420 XP, Level 9 at 500 XP
- **Level up timing** — Leveling occurs between scenarios when your accumulated XP reaches the next threshold
- **New ability cards** — At each level, choose 1 of 2 new ability cards for that level to add to your pool
- **HP increase** — Maximum HP increases according to your class's level chart
- **Perk checkmark** — Gain 1 additional perk checkmark each time you level up
- **Gold bonus** — Gain bonus gold equal to 15 × (new level) when leveling up

#### City & Road Events
- **Road events** — Drawn when traveling to a scenario location that is not in Gloomhaven or directly linked from the current location
- **City events** — Drawn when the party returns to Gloomhaven between scenarios
- **A/B choice** — Each event presents a narrative situation with two options; the party collectively decides, then flips the card for the result
- **Consequences** — Results can include gaining/losing gold, XP, reputation, items, blessings, curses, damage, conditions, or unlocking new events
- **Reputation effects** — Party reputation (ranging from -20 to +20) affects shop prices and event outcomes

#### Retirement & Unlocking
- **Personal quests** — Each character has a secret personal quest drawn at character creation defining their retirement condition
- **Retirement trigger** — When a character fulfills their personal quest requirement (e.g., kill X number of a specific enemy type, complete specific scenarios), they must retire
- **Unlock new class** — Retiring a character unlocks a new sealed character class box (one of the 11 locked classes), adding it to the party's options
- **Prosperity increase** — Each retirement increases Gloomhaven's prosperity by 1 checkmark
- **New character** — After retirement, the player creates a new character of any available class at a level equal to Gloomhaven's current prosperity level
- **Legacy stickers** — Apply map stickers and achievement stickers as directed to permanently modify the campaign world

#### Scenario Links
- **Branching paths** — Completing scenarios often unlocks multiple follow-up scenarios; some paths are mutually exclusive
- **Global achievements** — Certain scenario completions grant global achievements that open or close future scenario options
- **Party achievements** — Party-specific achievements track progress unique to your adventuring group
- **Side scenarios** — Many optional side scenarios provide rewards, items, and story content outside the main questline

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Scenario Victory
- **Complete the objective** — Each scenario has a specific goal listed in the scenario book, most commonly defeating all enemies in the final room
- **Alternate objectives** — Some scenarios require reaching a specific hex, surviving a number of rounds, looting specific treasures, escorting NPCs, or destroying objectives
- **Immediate end** — The scenario ends immediately when the objective is fulfilled; all remaining monsters are defeated and all money tokens on the map are looted by the closest character
- **Keep rewards** — All characters keep XP earned, gold looted, and treasure tiles found during the scenario
- **Scenario rewards** — The scenario book lists additional completion rewards: bonus XP, bonus gold, item unlocks, new scenario unlocks, achievements, and story progression

#### Scenario Failure
- **All exhausted** — If all characters are exhausted before the objective is complete, the scenario is failed
- **Keep partial rewards** — Players keep all XP earned and gold looted during the failed attempt
- **No scenario rewards** — Bonus completion rewards and achievements are not granted on failure
- **Replay allowed** — The scenario can be replayed immediately or at any later point; it remains available
- **No penalty** — There is no lasting campaign penalty for failing a scenario; you may adjust difficulty or composition before retrying

#### Campaign Progression
- **Main storyline** — The campaign follows a branching narrative across approximately 95 scenarios in the scenario book
- **Campaign victory** — Completing the main story arc's final scenario concludes the overarching narrative
- **Continuing play** — Even after the main story, players may continue playing remaining side scenarios, unlocking classes, and increasing prosperity
- **Difficulty scaling** — Scenario difficulty is set by the average party level divided by 2 (rounded up); players may adjust +1 or -1 from this recommended level
- **Prosperity milestones** — As Gloomhaven's prosperity increases, the shop expands with new items and new characters start at higher levels

subtopics.title: Beginner Tips
subtopics.content:
- **Conserve loss cards** — Your hand is your life clock; every lost card shortens how many rounds you can survive by reducing your total card pool
- **Count your rounds** — Calculate roughly how many rounds you can last: hand size divided by 2 (rounded down) is your maximum rounds without resting; plan accordingly
- **Save powerful losses for critical moments** — That devastating loss-action card is best played in the final room, not the first encounter
- **Coordinate initiative** — Talk with your party about whether you need to go fast or slow each round; going before monsters can prevent damage, going after allies lets you combo
- **Focus fire** — Concentrate attacks on one enemy to eliminate it rather than spreading damage across multiple targets; dead enemies deal no damage
- **Position defensively** — Avoid standing adjacent to multiple enemies if possible; being surrounded means taking multiple attacks per round
- **Use default actions** — Don't forget you can always use a default Move 2 (bottom) or Attack 2 (top) instead of a card's printed action if it's more useful
- **Loot intentionally** — Money tokens are your primary gold income for buying items; plan to loot when enemies drop coins
- **Learn your modifier deck** — Know what's in your deck; if you've already drawn the Miss card, your remaining attacks this shuffle cycle are safe
- **Read your personal quest early** — Understand your retirement goal from the beginning so you can work toward it naturally during play
- **Don't neglect items** — Spend your starting gold on useful items; a Stamina Potion (recover 2 discards) and Minor Healing Potion are excellent first purchases for most classes

subtopics.title: Key Concepts
subtopics.content:
- **Hand management is everything** — The central strategic tension is between using powerful loss cards for immediate impact versus preserving cards for longevity; this is the core skill of Gloomhaven
- **Initiative manipulation** — Choosing when to go fast (low initiative) to act before enemies or slow (high initiative) to let allies set up combos is critical every single round
- **Advantage/Disadvantage awareness** — Strengthen (Advantage) and Muddle (Disadvantage) dramatically shift attack modifier math; apply Strengthen to yourself before big attacks, Muddle to dangerous enemies
- **Element planning** — Generate elements on one turn to consume them on the next for enhanced effects; coordinate with allies who can consume elements you generate
- **Rest timing** — Knowing when to short rest vs. long rest is essential; long rests heal and refresh items but cost a full turn, short rests are free but randomly lose a card
- **Perk optimization** — Prioritize perks that remove -1 cards and add rolling positive modifiers; improving your modifier deck compounds in power over many scenarios
- **Enhancement investments** — Spend gold on permanent enhancements to ability cards between scenarios; adding elements, +1 damage, or conditions to frequently-used cards creates lasting power growth
- **Scenario level vs. difficulty** — Don't be afraid to lower the scenario level if your party is struggling; you still earn XP and gold, and completing the scenario is better than repeated failures
- **Party composition synergy** — Understand your role in the party; if you're the Brute, focus on tanking and crowd control, not trying to out-damage the Scoundrel
- **Treasure priority** — Numbered treasure tiles often contain unique items or scenario rewards; prioritize looting them before the scenario ends as they may be one-time opportunities

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
- **Burning loss cards early** — The most common new-player mistake is playing loss cards in the first room, then exhausting before reaching the final objective
- **Ignoring initiative coordination** — Failing to discuss initiative with your party leads to wasted turns, missed combos, and taking unnecessary damage
- **Forgetting to loot** — Gold is only earned by physically looting money tokens; if you don't pick up coins, you won't have gold for items between scenarios
- **Standing adjacent to multiple enemies** — Positioning near several enemies means taking multiple attacks each round; use doorways and obstacles as chokepoints
- **Neglecting elements** — Many ability cards have powerful enhanced effects when consuming elements; ignoring the element system leaves significant damage and utility on the table
- **Wasting healing on Poison/Wound** — Remember that healing a Poisoned or Wounded figure first removes the condition (consuming the heal) before restoring any HP; remove conditions strategically
- **Misunderstanding monster AI** — Monsters focus based on shortest path to attack, not simply closest distance; understanding focus rules helps you predict and manipulate enemy movement
- **Not using items** — Items like Stamina Potions (recover discarded cards) and Boots of Striding (+2 movement) are extremely powerful; forgetting to use them wastes gold and advantage
- **Overvaluing XP** — Chasing every XP-granting action instead of playing optimally for the scenario can lead to failure; XP matters, but scenario success matters more
- **Ignoring battle goals** — Battle goals are free perk checkmarks that permanently improve your modifier deck; try to complete them when feasible without sacrificing the scenario
- **Spreading damage** — Attacking different enemies for small amounts of damage leaves many enemies alive to attack you; focus fire to eliminate threats efficiently

metadata.notes: Comprehensive rules from Gloomhaven base game rulebook.
